claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is partially artifactual, caused by consistent scribal abbreviation or word-compounding conventions where morphological suffixes are concatenated inconsistently, such that word-final glyph sequences 'y', 'n', 'l', 'r' function as detachable morphological markers â€” splitting words on these terminals would reduce unique vocabulary by at least 25%.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph distribution is dominated by 'y' (most common), 'n', 'l', 'r', 'o' â€” highly skewed toward a small set
Average word length 5.03 is consistent with stem+suffix structure in Latin or Italian morphology
Top words 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy', 'chol' share terminal sequences suggesting productive morphological patterns
Word entropy 10.45 bits would decrease substantially if vocabulary is inflated by morphological variation
evidence_against: Simple positional rules already noted (belief 0.116) may already account for terminal glyph skew
If suffixes were detachable, word-medial forms of the same stems should appear â€” their frequency distribution would need to match expected stem frequencies
test: Segment all words by stripping the most frequent word-final glyphs ('y','n','l','r') as candidate suffixes; compute vocabulary size reduction ratio and test whether resulting stem vocabulary fits a Zipf distribution better (higher R-squared) than original word vocabulary
test_metric: Zipf R-squared for stem vocabulary vs. original word vocabulary; percentage reduction in hapax count after suffix stripping
test_threshold: R-squared improvement above 0.03 and hapax reduction above 25% would support morphological suffix hypothesis; improvement below 0.01 would argue against

tests_run.test: segment all words by stripping the most frequent word-final glyphs ('y','n','l','r') as candidate su
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If the hapax inflation is morphological rather than cipher-induced, this substantially changes decipherment strategy toward morphological analysis rather than pure substitution-cipher approaches, making it a high-value discriminating test.
